title: Market Analysis Methodology — Multi-Signal Narrative Framework
overview: Market analysis has two audiences: traders who need actionable reads, and agents who need structured data. This methodology bridges both. It combines order book microstructure, open interest positioning, funding rate divergence, liquidation clusters, and narrative catalysts into a unified assessment. The core principle: no single signal tells the story. The confluence of funding divergence + order book imbalance + OI/price divergence + liquidation cluster proximity is the story. When these signals agree, confidence is high. When they disagree, the market is confused — and that's the most important signal of all.
core_methodology: 1. ORDER BOOK IMBALANCE: Compute bid/ask depth ratio at top 10 levels. Ratio > 0.6 → bid_heavy (upward pressure). Ratio < 0.4 → ask_heavy (downward pressure). Pressure_strength = abs(ratio - 0.5) * 2. Warning: large resting orders at top of book can be spoofed — flag if top bid > 3x median bid. 2. OPEN INTEREST/MOMENTUM DIVERGENCE: 4h rolling correlation of price change vs OI change. Price up + OI down → bearish divergence (short covering rally, not new buying). Price down + OI up → bullish divergence (new shorts being opened, expect sharp rally when they cover). Price + OI same direction → conviction-driven trend. 3. FUNDING RATE DIVERGENCE: Compare funding across exchanges. If one exchange has extreme funding (positive or negative), assess whether it's driven by OI imbalance or temporary liquidity. Extreme funding > 0.1% annualized → unsustainably high, expect mean reversion. 4. LIQUIDATION CLUSTERS: Assess whether clustered liquidations represent exhausted forced selling (reversal) or cascade start (continuation), using cluster size and time-density. Identify nearest untested liquidity levels above and below. 5. NARRATIVE CATALYST: Every price move has a story. The story must reference a specific catalyst (not 'market is bullish'), have a price target where the catalyst is fully priced in, and a price target where the catalyst is proven false. The confidence band is the range between these targets.
evaluation_framework: SIGNAL CONFLUENCE: Score each signal's direction (-1 bearish, 0 neutral, +1 bullish). Weight by signal reliability (order book: 0.3, OI divergence: 0.25, funding: 0.2, liquidations: 0.15, narrative: 0.1). Composite score = weighted sum. If |composite| > 0.5 AND >3 signals agree → high conviction. If |composite| < 0.3 → no clear signal. If signals strongly disagree (some +1, some -1) → the market is confused — this is actionable: expect volatility, reduce position sizes, wait for re-convergence. NARRATIVE VALIDATION: A narrative is valid if it (a) references a specific catalyst, (b) specifies a price where the catalyst is priced in, (c) specifies a price where the catalyst is disproven, and (d) has a confidence band. Narratives without these four elements are noise, not analysis.
implementation_notes: The order book imbalance and liquidation cluster analysis require L2 data. Our HL L2 collector captures this. The funding rate divergence and OI analysis use public HL API endpoints. The narrative catalyst is the most subjective component — it benefits from multiple independent assessments (the maker-checker pattern from agent orchestration applies here). For agent consumption: output structured JSON with all signal scores, the composite, and the narrative. For human consumption: output the narrative first, then the supporting evidence. Agents read JSON. Humans read stories.
common_failure_modes: 1. NARRATIVE-FIRST ANALYSIS: 'I think the market is going up, let me find signals that confirm it.' The narrative should emerge from the signals, not precede them. Fix: compute all signal scores before writing the narrative. 2. OVERWEIGHTING ONE SIGNAL: 'The order book is bid-heavy, I'm bullish.' The order book shows resting orders, not committed orders. Spoofing is real. Fix: weight signals, don't trust any single one. 3. IGNORING CONFUSION: When signals disagree, the analyst picks the ones they like and ignores the rest. Disagreement IS the signal — it means the market lacks conviction. Fix: report the confusion explicitly. 4. VAGUE NARRATIVES: 'The market is bullish due to positive sentiment.' That's not analysis — it's mood. Fix: every narrative must name a specific catalyst and a falsifiable price target. 5. LIQUIDATION CASCADE BLINDNESS: Assuming a liquidation cluster is exhausted when it's actually the start of a cascade. The key differentiator is whether the cluster is growing (new liquidations appearing at nearby prices) or static (same cluster, no new additions). Growing = cascade. Static = exhausted.
regime_considerations: TRENDING UP: Order book imbalance is more reliable — trends attract momentum traders who place resting orders in the trend direction. OI divergence is critical — rising OI in an uptrend confirms conviction. Funding rate: watch for extreme positive funding (perps leading spot) — this is a warning, not a trade signal. RANGING: Order book signals degrade — resting orders at range boundaries are more likely to be canceled. OI divergence is the best signal in ranges — look for OI building at range extremes (positioning for breakout). Liquidation clusters form at range boundaries. HIGH VOL: All microstructure signals degrade — the order book is too thin and fast to trust. Funding rate divergence is the most reliable signal in high vol (extreme funding attracts arbitrageurs). Narrative catalysts dominate — in high vol, the story drives the price more than the order book. LOW VOL: Order book signals are most reliable — thin books mean resting orders represent genuine intent. OI building in low vol is a positioning signal — someone is accumulating before the breakout.

prompts.title: Funding Rate Divergence Narrative
prompts.prompt: Given funding rate data across exchanges ({exchange, funding_rate, funding_interval_hours, open_interest}), write 2-3 sentence trader narrative: which exchange has extreme funding, OI-imbalance or liquidity-driven, mean-reversion or continuation implication. One hedge word max. End with confidence: high/medium/low.

prompts.title: Liquidation Cluster Risk Read
prompts.prompt: Given liquidation events (price, side, size_usd, timestamp) near a price level: exhausted (reversal) or cascade (continuation)? Use size and time-density. Nearest untested liquidity above and below. One-line risk note for trader positioned in cluster direction. Ground every claim in specific numbers.

prompts.title: Order Book Imbalance Signal
prompts.prompt: Given L2 order book (bid/ask levels with price, size). Imbalance = sum(top_10_bids) / (sum(top_10_bids) + sum(top_10_asks)). >0.6 → bid_heavy. <0.4 → ask_heavy. Pressure_strength = abs(imbalance-0.5)*2. Expected_move_bps = pressure_strength * spread * 0.5. Flag if top bid > 3x median bid → spoofing risk. Uses same data as our HL L2 collector.

prompts.title: Open Interest/Momentum Divergence
prompts.prompt: Given hourly price and OI over 8h window. 4h rolling correlation: price up + OI down → bearish (short covering rally). Price down + OI up → bullish (new shorts, expect sharp covering rally). Price + OI same direction → conviction trend. Classic Wyckoff/VSA adapted for perpetual futures.

prompts.title: Narrative-Driven Price Target
prompts.prompt: Given news event, market positioning, and recent price action. Output: narrative (1 sentence, specific catalyst), bullish_target (catalyst fully priced in), bearish_target (catalyst proven false), most_likely_target (weighted by positioning), confidence_band [bullish, bearish], narrative_risk (one thing that invalidates). Single-point predictions are performative. Confidence bands are analytical.
